panels.description: Medium shot: Elizabeth and Mrs. Gardiner seated in a tastefully furnished drawing-room, early afternoon light filtering through a window. Elizabeth looks pensive, perhaps a hand resting on her chin, while Mrs. Gardiner looks at her with a gentle, understanding expression. The background shows hints of a refined but quiet domestic setting, reflecting the somber mood regarding Jane's state.

bubbles.text: Their first subject was her sister; and Elizabeth was grieved to hear of Jane's periods of dejection. Mrs. Gardiner also shared particulars of Miss Bingley's visit, proving Jane had, from her heart, given up the acquaintance.

panels.description: Medium shot: Mrs. Gardiner, with a subtle, knowing smile, leans forward slightly, initiating the conversation about Wickham's desertion. Elizabeth looks at her with a slightly surprised or wary expression, anticipating the next topic. The cozy drawing-room setting continues.

bubbles.text: But, my dear Elizabeth, what sort of girl is Miss King? I should be sorry to think our friend mercenary. If you will only tell me what sort of girl Miss King is, I shall know what to think.

panels.description: Medium close-up: A split panel focusing on their expressions. Elizabeth on the left, looking a bit defensive and uncomfortable, eyes slightly averted as she speaks. Mrs. Gardiner on the right, her expression one of mild skepticism and curiosity, awaiting a more revealing answer. Soft shadows cling to Elizabeth's face.

bubbles.text: She is a very good kind of girl, I believe. I know no harm of her.
But he paid her not the smallest attention till her grandfather's death made her mistress of this fortune?

panels.description: Medium shot: Elizabeth, asserting her points with a pragmatic, almost cynical air, perhaps with a slight shrug or a dismissive hand gesture. She looks directly at Mrs. Gardiner. Mrs. Gardiner looks on with a hint of disapproval, her posture firm. The exchange is spirited, with balanced lighting, suggesting a lively debate.

bubbles.text: No--why should he? If it were not allowable for him to gain _my_ affections, because I had no money, what occasion could there be for making love to a girl whom he did not care about, and who was equally poor?
But there seems indelicacy in directing his attentions towards her so soon after this event.
A man in distressed circumstances has not time for all those elegant decorums which other people may observe. If _she_ does not object to it, why should _we_?

panels.description: Medium close-up: Elizabeth's face, a sarcastic smirk playing on her lips, her eyes holding a deeper, possibly hurt, emotion. Mrs. Gardiner watches her closely from off-panel or in a slight background blur, a gentle, knowing smile now, having seen through Elizabeth's bravado. Lighting emphasizes the expressions, with some play of light and shadow on Elizabeth's face.

bubbles.text: No, Lizzy, that is what I do _not_ choose. I should be sorry, you know, to think ill of a young man who has lived so long in Derbyshire.

Oh, if that is all, I have a very poor opinion of young men who live in Derbyshire; and their intimate friends who live in Hertfordshire are not much better. Stupid men are the only ones worth knowing, after all.
Take care, Lizzy; that speech savours strongly of disappointment.

panels.description: Medium shot: Mrs. Gardiner holding out a small, decorative map or travel brochure with a warm, inviting smile. Elizabeth's eyes are wide with surprise and dawning delight, her hands perhaps slightly raised in anticipation. The background subtly shifts, perhaps a brighter window or a more open feel, symbolizing a sudden change of mood and future promise.

bubbles.text: Before they were separated by the conclusion of the play, she had the unexpected happiness of an invitation to accompany her uncle and aunt in a tour of pleasure which they proposed taking in the summer.

panels.description: Wide shot: Elizabeth stands dramatically in the drawing-room, arms outstretched as if embracing the world, a look of unbridled joy and excitement on her face, imagining grand landscapes of lakes and mountains. Mrs. Gardiner stands nearby, smiling fondly, observing Elizabeth's exuberant display. Bright, clear lighting bathes the scene, conveying immense hope and freedom.

bubbles.text: What a delight! What felicity! You give me fresh life and vigour. Adieu to disappointment and spleen. What are men to rocks and mountains? Oh, what hours of transport we shall spend! And when we _do_ return, it shall not be like other travellers, without being able to give one accurate idea of anything. We _will_ know where we have gone--we _will_ recollect what we have seen. Lakes, mountains, and rivers, shall not be jumbled together in our imaginations; nor, when we attempt to describe any particular scene, will we begin quarrelling about its relative situation. Let _our_ first effusions be less insupportable than those of the generality of travellers.
